content: 3DHISTECH Pannoramic 480 (P480) product page. The P480 is a high-throughput digital pathology slide scanner. Key specs: slide capacity up to 480 per batch; scan speed 40 seconds per slide at 20x, 0.24 micrometer per pixel, single-layer; throughput up to 90 slides per hour (depending on sample); fully automatic loading and scanning with 3DH/Sakura 20-slide baskets or Leica 30-slide baskets; optical options 20x NA 0.8, 10x NA 0.45, or 40x NA 0.95 objectives; 25 MP CIS CMOS camera; Hamamatsu 20W illumination; advanced features including multi-layer Z-stack scanning, polarization, and optional 40x water immersion; QC features including a safety container for problematic slides, fully automated AI-based tissue detection, and touch display with first-level QC; output in MRXS or DICOM format; physical 240 kg with an anti-vibration base.
provenance_note: Vendor product page. Methodology is Background (no formal analytical method — this is marketing and specification content). Topically Direct because the P480 is the exact vendor product anchoring the blueprint. Classified Direct x Background because it describes the target scanner precisely but contributes product parameters rather than simulation methodology. Marketing bias: throughput numbers are nominal best-case figures and should be adjusted downward for realistic operational modeling.

content: PMC lab automation review (PMC11062949). Comprehensive review of pathology laboratory automation. Identifies five canonical high-throughput clinical scanner families: NanoZoomer series (Hamamatsu), Aperio (Leica Biosystems), IntelliSite (Philips), Pannoramic series (3DHISTECH), and Axioscan (Zeiss). Structures pathology operations into three phases: pre-analytical (specimen collection, tissue processing, embedding, cutting, staining), analytical (diagnostic interpretation, reporting), and post-analytical (storage, archiving). Identifies embedding as one of the most critical steps — laborious and requiring trained specialists with manual dexterity — and material transfer between rack systems as another bottleneck. Provides one concrete upstream throughput reference: the AS-410M automated microtome produces approximately 250 blocks in a 7-hour shift with 24-hour capability. Emphasizes that standardization in tissue processing ensures uniformity by reducing discrepancies.
provenance_note: Review paper describing pathology lab automation broadly, including scanners as one component among tissue processors, microtomes, stainers, etc. Topically Adjacent because it provides structural context for the full pathology lab workflow surrounding the scanner, not specifically DES of scanner workflow. Methodology Background because it is a descriptive review, not a DES implementation. Useful for understanding upstream processes that feed the scanner and the overall lab structure (pre-analytical, analytical, post-analytical phases).
labels: pmc, pathology, automation, review, pmc11062949, upstream-context

content: Information aggregated from search snippets describing the Springer paper on batch scheduling in histopathology laboratories. Key findings: tissue processors are modeled as batch processing machines, with a 2-phased decomposition approach developed to improve the spread of workload and reduce tardiness. Using this decomposition method, peaks in histopathology workload may be reduced by up to 50% by better spreading the workload over the day, and turnaround times are reduced by up to 20% compared to current practices. A specific insight: performing tissue processing during the day, instead of only at night (except for priority specimens), had a significant effect on turnaround time, especially for small specimens. The paper itself is paywalled at Springer and was not directly fetched; all content here was aggregated from search engine result snippets describing the paper.
provenance_note: The source URL points to the Springer paper but the paper itself is paywalled and was never successfully fetched. All content here was aggregated from multiple search engine result snippets describing the paper. Unlike context rows 1 and 2 which are Background methodology (vendor marketing and review paper respectively), this row IS about a DES paper — the Springer paper uses simulation to model tissue processors as batch processing machines. Flagged for human follow-up via institutional Springer subscription.
